item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you read my reply! And replied to it, too.

+ Further thoughts!
- About stuff!

If you ever want someone to take a look at anything you write, please by all means give me a shout. Every unique voice is important in collaborative fiction! Especially when you're speaking to personal experience.

The thing about being a politician from Québec is a joke; dude got to my name about eighty years before I did. C'est la vie. I do have personal experience dealing with loved ones losing their sense of self, although my reaction was (and would probably continue to be) the opposite of Dr. Scout's; I never give up hope, and choose support over finality. It seemed appropriate for these two characters to reach the conclusions they did, but it's important, very important for me to emphasize that it's Rydderech's right to choose for himself that matters.

I do have obsessive compulsive disorder, which informs some of the mental tribulations I put my characters through. That doesn't give me context for other people's conditions, but it does put me in the mindset of wanting to explore how our minds work, and what our minds mean to ourselves and others.

I'll be mulling over your comments for some time. I edited the article furiously a few times before and after posting it, to try and make it clear that life is precious and valuable but people have the right to decide what to do with theirs; I actually edited it a bit more after reading your comment, to try and shy away from the idea that the depersonalization was Rydderech's main problem. The main problem is really that he's been sequestered underground, alone and unloved, while someone desperately wants to bring him home. I expect I'll go over it again to tighten that linkage; your comments have been very useful for that. So, you've likely helped me reinterpret my work in a way that will further improve it.

Every reader has a claim on stories that reflect their lives, so it's very meaningful for me to hear from someone with such an unlikely connection to the subject matter. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your unusually kind and thoughtful response. You have a gift for thoughtful writing, it seems. Some unsorted thoughts:

+ show block
– hide block
  • Given that your user page says, "Oh, hey, I'm [occupation] apparently! That's cool, I hope I'm good at it," I thought you were writing the dissociation from experience. It's impressive to learn that you weren't.
  • I've tried workshopping a tale in my head. I have more than enough different things I could write (letters, diary entries, phone calls, streams of thought, excerpts from reports on chemicals, conversations with imaginary therapists, etc.), but it's hard to determine the appropriate amount and variety to use, because I have no native understanding of what parts of dissociation are interesting to other people and what aren't. My experience is that it all is, to an extent, but I could write dissociative stuff endlessly, and I'm sure it gets boring to read at some point. (Probably somewhere around the length of SCP-3999.) I assume it's possible to construct an engaging narrative of arbitrary length from it, but the only inspiration I have for a narrative is chronicling how Dr Rydderech's perspective on reality changes over time, and that's not really a story. Or, it doesn't feel like a story to me; it feels like an autobiography.
  • I was aware of the lesson of the article when reading it… or, at least, became aware of it some time between reading it and writing my review. Nevertheless, the fact is that SCP-5520 is similar to me, so regardless of authorial intent, it was possible to apply the story's message to myself, and evaluate it on those terms. And while this is no fault of yours, it is representative of a very protective attitude towards continuity of identity that leads most people to be very scared of dissociation and identity death, which seems unwarranted to me. (Not that I'm necessarily right about that.) One way or the other, it's my own responsibility to reject death, and my reaction to this article is at most an iota in my constant conversation with the idea of what I can expect out of my life. You shouldn't feel responsible for any of that.
  • I'm not all that different from an elderly person, in some ways. I'm physically weak, thanks to chronic fatigue, and I have a bad essential tremor, thanks to meds. I'm older than my life expectancy, and alive mostly by chance. I've outlived some of my friends, and I am equally afraid of the death of my friends as of my own. My past is extremely distant to me; because of how poorly I remember it, I often say that it feels like I have lived thousands of years. I am not likely to ever grow more as a person or become more mature, though in my case, this has been true since my childhood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that was unexpected to say the least. Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me, and allowing yourself to be seen in this way. It was very brave, and it means a lot to me that you felt that connection.

Six thousand SCP files, and you find one that so closely parallels your feelings… how unlikely! Working through a problem like that and making a difference in the world is truly noble. Even if you've only described your contributions to science and society in abstract terms, know that they are worthwhile, as are you.

Your life has value, and as long as there's a spark in your soul there's a reason to live; the part with Scout's attempt was meant to be a commentary on assisted suicide and dignity for the elderly, not on suicide or euthanasia for the neurodivergent. I'm mentally ill myself, though not in the same way, so I would never have meant it like that.

But I'm very interested to hear that you saw yourself reflected in the bulk of this piece; I expected that to happen as little as you did! It must have come as a shock. I hope I handled the topic tastefully, and didn't make life more difficult for you. Thank you for reaching out, and you're more than welcome to write, draw, or do whatever comes to mind in relation to this SCP. I'd be pleased to see what you come up with, or even help, but no pressure. I recognize the difficulties you face, though I can only imagine what the experience is actually like.

Stay safe, and best of luck. We're all in the dark some times, but there's clarity in communication, so I'm glad we could have this conversation. Whether you remember it or not, I will, and you made a definite and lasting difference in my experience of this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Oh lord. I'm a protein scientist with a serious dissociative disorder, and I wasn't expecting to get made into a SCP. Huh. Dr Rydderech is so eerily relatable that I actually got some secondhand dissociation from reading his stuff. Or maybe my memories are lying… I don't know. I'm excited to read this again when I inevitably forget it exists in a few days, months, and years, repeatedly. I wonder if it'll feel any different in the distant future. The feeling of "Oh right, I've already read this," will be palpable, but it was already palpable this time, and I don't think I've already read it, since it's only two months old…

+ show block
– hide block

Um but yes, I don't do toxicology exactly (it's hard to say what I do… my coworkers are working on enzymes that stop a virus, but the support I provide is very abstract), but, "Hi I'm not sure whether time is real, also here's a terabyte of unsorted computational results," is definitely an accurate summary of my existence. I think I figured that I would never be represented ever in anything, because it's just too unlikely, so it's strange to be wrong, even if only for the length of an SCP article. But tomorrow I'll return to being right, I guess. I wonder if Dr Rydderech ever forgets he's a researcher. I've managed to forget my occupation a lot, but never my general purpose in life, which is broadly synonymous with the former. "Hi Viv, here's a report I wrote in my spare time on proactively fighting amyloid accumulation in neural tissue. I don't remember what I'm actually supposed to be doing, but it's okay, this is more important."

Sorry, I'm usually a little more coherent than this, I'm just not very present right now. I'm not sure if it's a result of reading the article. I read it between several minutes and several hours ago and that should be plenty of time to recover. But it's hard to determine cause and effect even on the best day, so I won't think about it too hard. I appreciate this article and I'll be sure to stumble upon the related tale eventually, when you write it.

edit: Upon further review and a slow drift towards lucidity, I came up with the idea of perhaps writing a tale from the perspective of this guy? Or maybe just drawing him, that seems more difficult to get offensively wrong. It would be roughly my first ever time drawing a human(oid) so it would be interesting. But maybe if it were possible for me to do that, I'd have done it by now, instead of apparently flaking out every time. Then again I only (re?)discovered my ability to draw this April. Sorry for rambling about dissociative stuff again, I'm just still so surprised to be "seen" like this, and I still feel compelled to share aspects of my life experience that align with what's represented in the article. Like, I thought qntm/sam512/Sam Hughes's antimemetics stuff represented my life well, what with the antimemetics researchers always having to assume they're forgetting everything, and developing inviolable rules to compensate for that, exactly as I've done, but… this is really on another level. (Or maybe I'm just not remembering/appreciating properly how impressed I was at Sam Hughes's stuff. That's always possible.)

edit 2: Oh also, no comment on the whole issue of leaving Dr Rydderech alive. Since Dr Scout knew him well for a very long time and was able to interpret his testimonials, I guess I'd go with what he said. But as someone who's a lot closer to SCP-5520 than they are to Dr Rydderech, I'm not allowing myself to entertain the idea that maybe it is better to be dead than what I am right now, which is something I struggle to reject rationally, and instead just reject categorically to save time and brain effort. Dr Rydderech is fictional and I'm only slightly less fictional, so… I'm not actually sure what point I was going to make with that, sorry. Anyway I don't feel like dying at the moment so I will refrain from further commentary.

edit 3: wait actually i remembered some legitimate reasons to live, and some things about my future that are predictable, even predictably positive. i don't remember the article anymore though so i still won't comment. sorry again.

Please collapse long posts as a courtesy to our mobile users. —RockTeethMothEyes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, that's so great to hear! And yeah, that's intentional - it feels "off" to present a transcript from the sixties containing such a high number, so I put that little bit about the amended number in to underscore the historicity. Did something similar with my Thaumiel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed reading this article in a way I haven't in a while. Your articles have been consistantly very interesting to read and I look forward to whatever else you have in store.
One question though: At one point in the article SCP-5520 is mentioned as being designated SCP-520 rather than 5520. Is this a typo or is the intention to indicate the age of the object and how its designation has changed over time? If so, that's some clever stuff, and the article was enjoyable either way!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, thanks so much! What a boost it is to hear all that.

I'm glad the structure and narrative worked for you, I had to assemble it piecemeal as the various concepts and the timeline occurred to my messy brain.

I can see the logs falling flat, they're more standard "spooky" in the midst of the emotional bits. Hopefully they still serve to break it up a little, so it's not just the saddest, oldest text message convo ever.

Very glad the ending works as intended, I played around with and revised it a lot. That extra last-minute effort was due to Grigori's feedback.

Thank you again for all the ego-stroking, and I hope to continue to not disappoint!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There’s a lot here.

There are a few authors who I’ll see have posted on LC and immediately go and read the articles, I’m happy to add your name to that ever growing list.

As for the actual article, this is phenomenal story telling. This almost felt like a Series 1 or early Series 2 SCP in the best of ways, and I really don’t know why. Maybe it’s the dialogue or the structure, maybe the simplistic anomaly?Regardless, I enjoyed this front and back, especially those notes. Something I have trouble with and I think other authors have trouble with is attempting to write a believable cognitive breakdown, and this does a really good job of subtlety conveying that there’s something wrong here. Dr. Scout was a real highlight here too, good motivations and watching the hatred and grief build up was great.

Those exploration logs were solid, although they were not the highlight for me and fell slightly flat, but I see their use in the narrative and they didn’t go on long enough for me to knock anything.

I’d be amiss to not talk about the ending. At first I didn’t see a reason for the email following that final note, but what a punch in the gut was that final paragraph?

Again, another great job. I’m looking forward to your next post. +1

item.title: Re: Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much! You made my day with five minutes to spare.

It does build on the previous two skips, but is meant to stand alone as much as possible. I'm very glad you found it surreal and had a good time with it, and if you do have further thoughts in the future I'd be thrilled to hear them.

As it stands, I very much appreciate the helpful feedback and kind words.

item.title: Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
This is so long that I am not presently able to wrap together a crit for you but I hope to return. What I will say is that this is a really good journey that I enjoyed a lot. I'm sure there's more I could know if I were more adept or read up at the series but there was plenty here that bordered on the surreal for me to just…really enjoy reading. I don't have much more succinct feedback than that at this time.

Really good work. I was skeptical at the jargon upon reading the image caption and seeing "Archon" but the astute and direct nature of the ConProcs brought me right back in. Very glad I stayed.

item.title: Dignity
item.content:encoded:
There is meaning in your lives. You are loved, you are wanted, you are appreciated. And you are unique. The variety of experiences which constitute our race is beautiful. You are valid, you are meaningful, and you deserve life. Live it on your own terms, and accept no definitions, no impositions, but your own. Only you know who you are. Revel in the knowledge.

And that's the end of my Site-43 history trilogy. It's not the end of history, though. So that's nice.

It's also probably the last thing you'll hear about Site-43 until the end of the Canon Renaissance Contest. I'm sure you'll survive. Not hearing about Site-43, that is; whether or not you survive the Canon Renaissance Contest depends on a lot more variables.

I could have twisted the knife harder, but some of this material is very personal to me, so I didn't. When I eventually write the companion tale, I'll be sure to absolutely devastate myself for you.

Many thanks to Grigori Karpin for his crit, and an excellent suggestion about the ending (that there should in fact be one).
